name: Visited Set
summary: Walk the list once, marking every node visited; if we ever step onto an already-visited node, there is a cycle.
visualizationIntent: init: build the linked list from the values, wiring each node to the next and linking the tail back to pos when there is a cycle (a visible back-edge); the visited marks all start off and cur sits at the head. loop: cur walks one node at a time along the chain. check: before marking, test whether cur is already visited. update: mark the current node visited (it lights up) — if a node is reached that was already visited, flash it and report a cycle. move: cur follows the next pointer to the following node, or the null sentinel when the list ends. return: show the boolean answer; the trail of visited nodes is the O(n) memory this approach spends.

lineExplanations.3: Count how many nodes the list has.
lineExplanations.4: Start an empty next-pointer array (nxt[i] = the id the i-th node points to).
lineExplanations.5: Loop over every node to wire up its next pointer.
lineExplanations.6: For every node except the last, the next node is simply the following index.
lineExplanations.7: Point node i at node i+1.
lineExplanations.9: The tail points at pos — the cycle target, or -1 for no cycle.
lineExplanations.10: Start an empty visited-flag array — this is the extra O(n) memory.
lineExplanations.11: Loop over every node to give it a visited flag.
lineExplanations.13: cur starts at the head (id 0); -1 marks 'no node' for an empty list.
lineExplanations.14: found records the answer; it stays False until we revisit a node.
lineExplanations.15: Keep walking while cur points at a real node.
lineExplanations.16: If the current node was already visited, we've looped back — a cycle.
lineExplanations.17: Record that a cycle was found.
lineExplanations.18: Stop early — the answer is settled.
lineExplanations.19: Mark the current node visited so a later revisit is detected.
lineExplanations.20: Follow the next pointer to the next node (or -1 at the end).
lineExplanations.21: Return whether a cycle was found.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def hasCycle(self, values, pos): values are the node values; pos is the cycle target index (-1 = none).
syntaxExplanations.5: for i in range(n): loops i over each node index.
syntaxExplanations.6: if i < n - 1: true for every node except the last.
syntaxExplanations.7: nxt.append(i + 1) records that node i points to node i+1.
syntaxExplanations.8: else: handles the final node (bare else — no step of its own).
syntaxExplanations.9: nxt.append(pos) records the tail's next as pos (the cycle link, or -1).
syntaxExplanations.11: for i in range(n): loops i over each node index.
syntaxExplanations.12: visited.append(False) marks node i unvisited to start.
syntaxExplanations.13: cur = 0 if n > 0 else -1 — a ternary; -1 is the null sentinel for an empty list.
syntaxExplanations.14: found = False initializes the boolean answer.
syntaxExplanations.15: while cur != -1: keep walking until we fall off the end.
syntaxExplanations.16: if visited[cur]: has this node been seen before?
syntaxExplanations.17: found = True marks that we revisited a node — a cycle exists.
syntaxExplanations.19: visited[cur] = True records that this node has now been seen.
syntaxExplanations.20: cur = nxt[cur] follows the next pointer to the next node.
syntaxExplanations.21: return found hands back the boolean answer.
